body: Resident walked slower than baseline after lunch; gait looked cautious near the hallway turn.
Resident refused the evening medication and said, 'I do not want it tonight.'
Noise from the corridor seemed to agitate the resident; they asked staff to keep the room quiet.
Gait remained slower than baseline but steady with walker support.
Resident again refused medication and repeated, 'I do not want it tonight.'
My mother has always loved quiet music after dinner - it used to help her wind down even before she moved in, and I still see her hum along when the room is calm.
She has always liked warm tea after meals, ever since I was a child. It was our little ritual together, and she still brightens up when it's offered after lunch or dinner.
Evening medication administered on schedule after a familiar nurse walked through the sequence first. Resident accepted without hesitation.
Evening medication administered on schedule. Resident asked for the door to be partly closed first, then accepted the dose calmly.
Resident refused the evening medication and said, 'I do not want it tonight.' An unfamiliar staff member was covering the round. Re-offered later with a familiar nurse and resident accepted.

Evening agitation observed near the corridor by the room; increased noise from a passing cart appeared to trigger restlessness. Staff lowered noise, sat at eye level, and resident settled within a few minutes.

Corridor noise from evening cleaning equipment coincided with visible agitation; resident asked staff to keep the room quiet. Situation resolved after staff reduced nearby noise and slowed their approach.
Resident rested comfortably after lunch, as is typical, and preferred an unhurried transition back to her chair by the window.
Gait was steady with walker support during the daytime routine; resident responded well to short sentences and either-or choices offered during the walk.
